participants.extracted: Eligible participants were adults aged 18–60 years who smoked at least 10 cigarettes per day, had less than 3 months of abstinence in the past year, and were interested in quitting smoking. Individuals were excluded if they were using psychoactive medications, had a serious or unstable medical condition within the prior 6 months, or met DSM-IV criteria for another substance dependence in the past year. Participants were recruited through flyers and media advertisements. Of 103 eligible individuals, 88 were randomized, and 87 were included in the baseline sample; 45% were ethnic minorities and 63% were men, with a mean age of about 46 years. On average, participants smoked 20 cigarettes per day, began smoking regularly at age 16, and had 5.2 prior quit attempts.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to one of two group-based behavioral interventions delivered over four weeks with eight total sessions (twice weekly), matched for session length, quit date timing, and home-practice materials. The experimental intervention was Mindfulness Training (MT), adapted for smoking cessation, which emphasized moment-to-moment awareness and acceptance of cravings, affect, and withdrawal. MT sessions introduced the associative learning model of smoking, taught practices such as recognizing, accepting, investigating, and noting cravings (RAIN), loving-kindness meditation, awareness of breath, mindful walking, and mindfulness in daily activities and smoking, with participants receiving a meditation practice CD for home practice. The comparison intervention was Freedom From Smoking (FFS), a manualized smoking cessation program that included behavior modification, stress reduction, relapse prevention, self-monitoring of smoking patterns, identification of triggers, development of a quit plan, coping strategies, lifestyle change, social support, and relaxation techniques, with a practice CD for home use. Participants in either arm were neither encouraged nor discouraged from using nicotine replacement therapy during treatment or follow-up.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure smoking cessation outcomes, specifically 1-week point prevalence abstinence and average number of cigarettes smoked per day. These were designated as the primary endpoints at treatment completion (week 4) and at 17 weeks after treatment initiation, with additional follow-up assessments conducted at 6 and 12 weeks after treatment initiation. Abstinence was assessed by self-report using the Timeline Follow Back method and verified by exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) ≤10 parts per million. The study also planned secondary analyses of home-practice–outcome associations within the mindfulness group, examining correlations between amount/type of home practice and smoking outcomes (point prevalence abstinence and cigarettes smoked per day) at weeks 4, 6, 12, and 17.
